retrieved_docs.title: Melanotic Neuroectodermal Tumor of Infancy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Melanotic neuroectodermal tumor of infancy (MNTI) is a rapidly growing benign tumor that arises from the neural crest. It typically occurs in infants in the head and neck region. It was described for the first time in 1918 by Krompecher, and since the origin was unknown, it was described as congenital melanocarcinoma. Until 1966, this tumor was characterized by variable names (e.g., retinal anlage tumor, pigmented congenital epulis, melanotic progonoma, pigmented teratoma, atypical melanoblastoma, etc.). However, Borello and Gorlin found that the tumor produces vanillylmandelic acid (VMA), which is produced by other types of tumors that arise from the neural crest. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Melanotic Neuroectodermal Tumor of Infancy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the epidemiology of melanotic neuroectodermal tumor of infancy. Review the appropriate steps for the evaluation of melanotic neuroectodermal tumor of infancy. Outline the management options available for melanotic neuroectodermal tumor of infancy. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance the care of melanotic neuroectodermal tumor of Infancy and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Melanotic Neuroectodermal Tumor of Infancy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: MNTI has had 500 cases reported since 1918. The highest prevalence is in the United States, followed by India. There is a slightly higher predominance in males. [3] Since this tumor rarely metastasizes to distant locations, surgical excision is considered the best treatment. However, local recurrence is common. MNTI is considered a locally aggressive tumor because it has a high growth rate. Even though the neoplasm is considered to be of neuroblastic origin because it produces VMA, it is not a common feature. A recent systematic review of jaw lesions found that only 35% of cases had high VMA levels. [1] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Melanotic Neuroectodermal Tumor of Infancy -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Melanotic neuroectodermal tumor of infancy (MNTI) is a rapidly growing benign tumor arising from the neural crest cells. It typically affects infants and occurs in the head and neck region. MNTI has a total of 500 cases reported since 1918, and it can cause significant morbidity. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of melanotic neuroectodermal tumor of infancy and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in the care of patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Melanotic Neuroectodermal Tumor of Infancy -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The metastasis rate for this neoplasm is approximately 3%. This neoplasm is a fast-growing tumor, leading to an aggressive compression of adjacent structures. As such, the excision of the tumor is the most common modality of treatment, and local recurrence was 20%. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Melanotic Neuroectodermal Tumor of Infancy -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The extremely low incidence of MNTI usually makes it a missed differential diagnosis. Lack of familiarity with MNTI leads to misdiagnosis and delay. Delay in diagnosis leads to local tissue invasion.

retrieved_docs.title: Melanotic Neuroectodermal Tumor of Infancy -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: MNTI management is based on reports which demonstrate that complete excision is the best treatment. A 2019 systematic review analyzing the treatment modalities for MNTI, demonstrated that excision appears to be the best choice as it is associated with low recurrence and lower morbidity rates. [4] A few cases were managed with neoadjuvant chemotherapy before surgery, which can decrease the need for wide margins resection, hence decreasing the disfiguring sequences of the surgery. [25] [26] Still, excision either with or without additional treatment has quite a high rate of recurrence (22%). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Melanotic Neuroectodermal Tumor of Infancy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of MNTI requires a very high index of suspicion. The diagnosis of MNTI must be considered in any rapid-growing mass, especially in the head and neck region. Patients with MNTI are best managed by an interprofessional team approach. Follow-up with the patient is important to detect any recurrence at the early stages of the disease and avoid delaying the diagnosis and its complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Melanotic Neuroectodermal Tumor of Infancy -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographically, MNTI appears as a hypodense lesion on computed tomography (CT) scans. However, the melanin component of the soft tissue in MNTI will appear as a hyperdense finding. Enlargement of the lesion will lead to the destruction of the bony structures, and this will appear as a "sun-burst" result on the imaging. Therefore, a CT scan is a good choice to determine the extent of the neoplasm for surgical planning. [23] Ultrasonography for peripheral lesions has been performed in a small number of cases. On ultrasound, MNTI lesions appeared heterogeneous, well-demarcated, with decreased vascularization. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Melanotic Neuroectodermal Tumor of Infancy -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Although MNTI mostly presents as a benign neoplasm, malignant behavior is observed in 3% of cases. The 5-year recurrence rate is slightly higher in females than males (25% versus 22%). [9] Clinical and immunohistological presentation of the tumor can predict the prognosis and recurrence rate:

retrieved_docs.title: Melanotic Neuroectodermal Tumor of Infancy -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: MNTI is a rare tumor. According to the recent systematic reviews, there are only around 500 cases reported worldwide, with the highest reports from the United States, followed by India, Germany, and Brazil. Only 32 countries in the world reported MNTI cases. The prevalence of MNTI is slightly higher in males than females (1.3 to 1). Most reported cases are located in the head and neck region of infants with a mean age of 6.5 months. The maxilla (62%) is the most common site for this neoplasm, followed by the skull (15%) and the mandible (8%). However, in rare cases, it could arise in different locations other than the head and neck, such as ovaries, testis, femur, and others. [3] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Melanotic Neuroectodermal Tumor of Infancy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Despite the rarity of MNTI, which makes it difficult to assess the complication of this tumor, this rapidly growing mass may cause facial asymmetry and invade muscles, bony structures and act in a locally destructive manner. Metastasis is rare but has been reported in limited cases. Treatment also can lead to complications as well. For example, surgical excision of the mass may lead to local nerve and tissue injury as well as potential disfiguration. Chemotherapy may cause side effects such as ototoxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: Melanotic Neuroectodermal Tumor of Infancy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Melanotic neuroectodermal tumor of infancy is a rare neoplasm of neural crest cell etiology. Also, its microscopic features are consistent with this theory, as it appears as biphasic clustered cells. [1] On the genetic and molecular levels, there are few studies on MNTI. Oncogenic BRAFV600E mutation, which was previously known to be associated with melanoma, was reported in 3 cases of femur MNTI lesions. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Melanotic Neuroectodermal Tumor of Infancy -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Age: Presentation at an age less than 2-months is associated with higher recurrence. Paradoxically, a presentation greater than 12 months is associated with 75 fold mortality. [3] Distant metastasis at presentation is associated with higher recurrence rates and 14-fold higher mortality. [3] Location and size of the lesion: The mandible was found to have the highest recurrence rate (33%), followed by the skull (31%) and the maxilla (19%). [31] The larger size of the lesion (> 5cm) is also associated with a higher rate of recurrence. [16] Model of treatment: Curettage is associated with the highest recurrence rate (61%). [4] Immunohistochemical markers: Although it is rare, the expression of Ki-67 and CD99 on MNTI is associated with more aggressive behavior of the lesion. [32]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

Melanomas of the skin are identified as malignant skin cancer (سرطان الجلد الخبيث) and possess an inverse classification relationship with malignant lip cancer (سرطان الشفة الخبيث), highlighting overlapping pathological features within head‑and‑neck malignancies. Although primary melanomas arise on the skin, they can involve ocular structures, with the retina (ret) noted as a possible site of involvement, underscoring the potential for metastatic spread beyond the cutaneous origin. Together, these hierarchical (parent/child), associative (clinically associated with), and definitional (inverse_isa, mapped_from) relationships provide a comprehensive framework for understanding how primary tumors such as colon, thyroid, and cutaneous melanomas relate to their diverse metastatic manifestations across multiple anatomical sites, informing diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2565
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and are classified as a type of skin cancer. They are distinct from “سرطان الشفة الخبيث,” the Arabic term for malignant lip cancer, which represents a separate oncologic entity affecting the lip rather than the skin. Although one of the provided relationships incorrectly linked melanomas of skin to a finding site of “ret” (suggesting the retina), the correct anatomical location for cutaneous melanoma is the skin itself, not the retina. Consequently, melanomas of skin and malignant lip cancer differ in both their primary sites and their disease classifications, underscoring the importance of accurate anatomical and taxonomic identification in patient diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2178
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concept “سرطان الجلد الخبيث” (malignant skin cancer) is mapped from the broader description “ورم ثانوي خبيث في مواضع محددة أخرى” (malignant secondary tumor in other specific locations), indicating that malignant skin cancer is understood as a form of malignant secondary tumor that can arise in particular anatomical sites. This relationship clarifies that the skin manifestation of a malignant secondary tumor is classified under the specific term “سرطان الجلد الخبيث,” linking the general category of secondary malignant neoplasms to its specific cutaneous presentation.
